skill.name: shellcheck-configuration
skill.description: Master ShellCheck static analysis configuration and usage for shell script quality. Use when setting up linting infrastructure, fixing code issues, or ensuring script portability.
skill.summary: Master ShellCheck static analysis configuration and usage for shell script quality. Use when setting...
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 111 static findings are false positives. The skill contains shell command examples used to demonstrate ShellCheck error codes (SC2086, SC1091, etc.). The scanner misidentified 'SC' as 'C2' keywords. URLs point to legitimate ShellCheck project resources. References to ~/.shellcheckrc and ~/.bashrc are standard config file paths, not malicious file access. The skill is purely educational documentation for ShellCheck static analysis tool.

high_findings.title: False Positive: Scanner misidentified ShellCheck error codes as C2 keywords
high_findings.description: The static scanner flagged 'C2 keywords' at multiple lines (80, 110, 113, 116, etc.), but these are actually ShellCheck error codes like SC2086, SC1091, SC2119. The scanner misread 'SC' as 'C2'. This is a documentation skill teaching users about ShellCheck error codes - no actual C2/malware content exists.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Pattern match on 'SC###' codes is clearly ShellCheck error reference format. Context confirms these are documentation examples of error codes to teach users how to fix shell script issues.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: Shell command examples are documentation content, not execution
medium_findings.description: The static scanner flagged 'external_commands' (73 occurrences) showing backticks, $(), and shell invocations. These are example shell script patterns shown in the skill to demonstrate what ShellCheck will detect. The skill is teaching users about ShellCheck - not executing these commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All shell commands are shown as markdown code blocks within documentation. No actual command execution occurs. These are examples of problematic shell patterns that ShellCheck would flag.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: Network URLs are legitimate ShellCheck project resources
medium_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to github.com/koalaman/shellcheck and shellcheck.net - the official ShellCheck project and documentation. These are appropriate resources for a ShellCheck configuration skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs are to the legitimate ShellCheck open-source project on GitHub and its official wiki. No suspicious network activity.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: Filesystem references are standard shell config paths
medium_findings.description: References to ~/.shellcheckrc and ~/.bashrc are standard configuration file paths in shell scripting. These are not malicious file access attempts - they are example configuration file locations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: ~/.shellcheckrc is ShellCheck's standard config file location. ~/.bashrc is a common shell rc file. Both are appropriate examples for a shellcheck configuration skill.

low_findings.title: False Positive: sudo apt-get install is legitimate installation instruction
low_findings.description: Line 263 shows 'sudo apt-get install shellcheck' which is the standard way to install ShellCheck on Debian/Ubuntu systems. This is not privilege escalation for malicious purposes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Installing shellcheck via apt-get is the documented installation method. This is a standard, legitimate system administration command.

content.user_title: Configure ShellCheck for Shell Script Quality
content.value_statement: Shell scripts often contain subtle bugs and portability issues that are hard to catch manually. This skill provides comprehensive guidance on configuring and using ShellCheck to automatically detect and fix shell script problems.
actual_capabilities: Configure ShellCheck for different shell dialects (bash, sh, dash, ksh)
Integrate ShellCheck into CI/CD pipelines and pre-commit hooks
Understand and fix common ShellCheck error codes (SC1000-SC3999)
Suppress false positives with inline directives
Use ShellCheck output formats (default, gcc, json, quiet)
limitations: Requires ShellCheck to be installed on the system
Does not execute shell scripts - only performs static analysis
Cannot catch runtime logic errors or environmental issues
Limited to POSIX-compatible shell syntax analysis

use_cases.title: DevOps Engineer: CI/CD Pipeline Setup
use_cases.description: Set up automated shell script linting in GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, or other CI/CD systems to catch issues before deployment.

use_cases.title: Software Developer: Script Quality Improvement
use_cases.description: Fix existing shell script errors and improve portability across different Unix/Linux environments using ShellCheck guidance.

use_cases.title: System Administrator: Team Code Standards
use_cases.description: Enforce consistent code quality standards across team scripts with project-level configuration and pre-commit validation.

output_examples.input: How do I fix SC2086 in: for i in $list; do echo $i; done
output_examples.output: SC2086 warns about unquoted variables causing word splitting. Fix: use "$list" or "${list[@]}" for arrays. Corrected: for i in "$list"; do echo "$i"; done

output_examples.input: Create a .shellcheckrc for strict Bash validation
output_examples.output: Configuration that sets shell=bash, enables optional checks like require-variable-braces, and disables SC1091 (sourced files not found) as a project default.

output_examples.input: What are the most common ShellCheck errors to fix?
output_examples.output: Top 5: SC2086 (unquoted variables), SC1091 (sourced files), SC2154 (unset variables), SC2148 (tips), SC2164 (use cd with || exit). Each has specific fixes detailed in the skill.

best_practices: Run ShellCheck in CI/CD pipelines to catch issues before merging
Configure the target shell correctly - do not analyze Bash scripts as POSIX sh
Document why specific warnings are suppressed using inline directives
anti_patterns: Disabling warnings without understanding why they appear
Analyzing scripts for wrong shell dialect (Bash as sh)
Ignoring ShellCheck output without addressing the underlying issue

faq.question: What is ShellCheck?
faq.answer: ShellCheck is a static analysis tool that finds bugs, warnings, and issues in shell scripts. It supports Bash, sh, dash, ksh, and other POSIX shells.

faq.question: How do I install ShellCheck?
faq.answer: On macOS: brew install shellcheck. On Ubuntu/Debian: apt-get install shellcheck. Or build from source at github.com/koalaman/shellcheck.

faq.question: What do ShellCheck error codes mean?
faq.answer: Codes are grouped: SC1000-1099 (parser errors), SC2000-2099 (shell issues), SC2100-2199 (quoting), SC3000-3999 (POSIX compliance). Each code has specific guidance.

faq.question: How do I suppress specific warnings?
faq.answer: Use inline directives: # shellcheck disable=SC2086. Or configure .shellcheckrc with disable=SC2086 to suppress project-wide.

faq.question: Can ShellCheck catch security issues?
faq.answer: ShellCheck detects some security-related patterns like unquoted variables, but it is not a security scanner. Use dedicated security tools for vulnerability assessment.

faq.question: How do I integrate ShellCheck with my editor?
faq.answer: Most editors have ShellCheck plugins: VS Code (vscode-shellcheck), Sublime Text, Vim (ale plugin), Emacs (flycheck). These provide real-time feedback.
